questions.question: An ingot's top-center shrinkage cavity ('pipe') is not completely removed by cropping before the ingot is rolled into bar. In the finished bar, the surviving pipe most likely appears as:
options: A ragged, branched surface crack at a section change
An elongated internal separation running in the rolling direction
Scattered rounded surface porosity
A smooth, curved surface line where two metal streams met
questions.explanation: Mechanical working stretches internal ingot discontinuities in the direction of working — surviving pipe becomes an internal, longitudinal separation, while branched cracks at section changes (hot tears) and smooth curved lines (cold shuts) are casting features.

questions.question: A crankshaft returns from service with suspected fatigue cracking running transverse to the journal axis. Which magnetization is correct, and why?
options: Circular (head shot), because the flux circles the journal parallel to the crack
Longitudinal (coil shot), because flux along the axis crosses a transverse crack at about 90 degrees
Prods placed across the journal, because prods always find transverse cracks
The residual method, because fatigue cracks retain magnetism
questions.explanation: Detectability requires flux crossing the discontinuity at roughly 45–90°; a transverse crack runs around the shaft, so longitudinal flux from a coil crosses it, while circular flux runs parallel to it and produces little leakage.

questions.question: Several parts in a basket show faint, fuzzy, parallel arcs that vanish after the parts are demagnetized and retested. These indications were:
options: Relevant grinding cracks
Nonrelevant magnetic writing from contact with magnetized parts or tools
False indications from bath drainage
Subsurface stringers
questions.explanation: Magnetic writing — loosely held, fuzzy scribbles from contact with another magnetized object — is real leakage but not a discontinuity: it is nonrelevant, and its disappearance after demagnetization and retest confirms the call.

questions.question: For a visible (non-fluorescent) particle examination, the minimum white light intensity at the examination surface is typically:
options: About 2 footcandles
About 20 footcandles
About 100 footcandles (roughly 1,000 lux), verified with a calibrated meter
No requirement — visible particles work in any light
questions.explanation: Visible-particle work typically requires at least 100 fc (about 1,000 lux) of white light at the surface — measured, recorded, and traceable to a calibrated meter; the 2 fc figure is the ambient limit for fluorescent booths.

questions.question: A customer contract invokes a design code whose MT acceptance criteria differ from your shop's general procedure. During procedure development, which requirement governs?
options: The shop procedure, because the bench crew knows it best
The contract and its invoked code — requirements flow down, and the procedure must be tailored and approved to comply
Whichever document was printed most recently
The inspector's judgment on the day of the test
questions.explanation: The document hierarchy runs downhill from the design code/contract through standard practices to the company procedure and technique card; a conflicting procedure gets revised and re-approved, never the other way around.

questions.question: During validation of a new technique, where should QQI artificial-flaw shims be placed?
options: Anywhere flat and convenient, notch side up
Notch side toward the part surface at representative locations, including worst-case geometry such as fillets, section changes, and coil-shot ends
Only on the Ketos ring specimen
On a separate test bar instead of the actual part geometry
questions.explanation: QQIs are taped notch-side down on the real geometry, and the technique is qualified at the hardest locations — if the worst-case spot blooms, the easier ones will.

questions.question: A settling test on a visible wet bath reads 0.8 mL per 100 mL. Against the typical 1.2–2.4 mL window, the correct action is to:
options: Test parts — a thin bath is extra sensitive
Add particles, re-agitate thoroughly, and re-run the settling test before examining parts
Add carrier to dilute further
Switch the lamp to a higher intensity to compensate
questions.explanation: 0.8 mL is below the typical visible-bath window of 1.2–2.4 mL per 100 mL — a starved bath misses indications, so particles are added and the correction is proven with another settling test.

questions.question: Which settling-tube observation condemns a fluorescent bath outright by common practice, even when particle concentration reads in range?
options: The particle column reads 0.3 mL per 100 mL
A contamination band approaching 30% of the settled particle volume, or a carrier liquid that fluoresces noticeably under UV
The sample was drawn from the delivery hose
The tube needed the full 30-minute settling time
questions.explanation: Quantity is not quality: heavy contamination banding or a fluorescing carrier (stripped dye destroying contrast) replaces the bath regardless of an in-range concentration reading.

questions.question: A technique card specifies a coil shot on a 15-in. long, 3-in. diameter shaft using a 3-turn coil. Using NI ≈ 45,000/(L/D) as the starting point, the card's amperage should be near:
questions.explanation: L/D = 15/3 = 5, so NI ≈ 45,000/5 = 9,000 ampere-turns; with 3 turns that is 3,000 A — a starting value to be validated with shims and a Hall probe and written with a tolerance.

questions.question: Per the recording section of a well-written MT procedure, which set of report contents is required?
options: Only the accept/reject stamp — everything else is optional

Part identity, procedure number and revision, equipment and settings, particle type and batch, light-meter readings, findings with sizes and locations, disposition, and the technician's name, certification level, and date
Only photographs of rejected parts
The technician's initials and the shift number
questions.explanation: A defensible record reconstructs the examination: what was tested, to which revision, with what equipment and settings, under what verified conditions, what was found and decided, and by whom — retained per the quality system.
